content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Michael C. Ghizzoni 
County Counsel 
105 E. Anapamu Street, Suite 201 
Santa Barbara, CA 93 101 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-194 
Dear Mr. Ghizzoni: 
August 18, 2017 
This letter responds to your request on behalf of Santa Barbara County Supervisor Janet 
Wolf for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the 
"Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (/11 re Oglesby 
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
QUESTION 
Pursuant to the Political Refonn Act, may Supervisor Wolf make and participate in making 
County decisions regarding the fonnation of the Groundwater Sustainability Agency ("GSA") for 
areas of the Goleta Groundwater Basin where she owns a residential property? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Supervisor Wolf does not have a prohibited financial interest in the formation of the 
GSA under the Act. 
FACTS 
At a public meeting on August 22,2017, the County's Board of Supervisors is scheduled to 
vote on fonning a GSA for the Fringe Areas of the Basin located within Santa Barbara County, 
pursuant to the Sustainable Grountlwater Management Act ("SGMA"). SGMA provides a I 
framework for the sustainable management of groundwater supplies by local agencies and provides 
tools, authority, and a timeline for local agencies. (Water Code§ 10727.2.) SGMA requires 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-194 
Page No. 2 
sustainable management of basins, such as the Goleta Groundwater Basin, that are designated as 
medium- or high-priority by the California Department of Water Resources ("DWR"). (Water Code 
§ 10727.) If a GSA is fonned for the Fringe Areas of the Basin, the GSA will be responsible for 
developing a Ground Water Sustainability Plan ("GSP") for the affected area. In addition, the GSP 
may be carried out by "multiple plans implemented by multiple groundwater sustainability agencies 
and coordinated pursuant to a single coordination agreement that covers the entire basin." (Water 
Code§ 10727(b)(3).) 
According to a follow up telephone conversation with your office on August 17, 2017, it is 
the state, not the county, that declares the status of a groundwater basin, and that GSAs are required 
under state law. The GSA would not have land use and pennitting authority, or the authority to 
change fees for wells, but would provide input to the County on the sustainable management of 
groundwater resources. A GSA's powers do not supersede the County's land use authority or 
general plan. 
The Basin, as defined in DWR's Bulletin 118, is roughly 9,217 acres and the Fringe Area 
covers approximately 1 ,184 of those acres. In 2010, the population overlying the Basin was 47,252. 
The majority of the Basin's water users have undergone the lengthy legal process of adjudication, 
and a majority of the Basin is therefore not subject to most of the requirements of SOMA. (Water 
Code § 10720.8(a)(7)). However, the boundary of the adjudication does not completely match with 
the Basin boundary as defined in DWR's Bulletin 118, leaving Fringe Areas which are not managed 
pursuant to the adjudication. DWR has designated the Basin to be a medium-priority basin. SOMA 
requires that all portions of medium-priority and high-priority basins be managed, and a GSA is 
therefore required for these Fringe Areas. The Fringe Areas represent a very small portion of 
overall Goleta Basin water use and it is unclear what preparation of a GSP would accomplish in 
these areas. 
These Fringe Areas are primarily located north of Cathedral Oaks Road and include larger 
areas west of the Goleta city limits and along San Antonio Creek north and east of Tucker's Grove 
County Park. The Fringe Areas lie entirely within the boundary of the Goleta Water District, which 
is the only domestic and agricultural purveyor within the Basin. On June 14, 2017, the Goleta Water 
District Board voted not to fonn a GSA. The only other entity eligible to fonn a GSA-besides the 
County of Santa Barbara is the City of Goleta, which has expressed no interest in fonning, or being 
a part of, a GSA. In a follow-up telephone conversation with your office on August 17, 2017, you 
noted that the City of Goleta lies mostly above an already adjudicated groundwater basin. 
As the sole entity fonning the GSA, the Santa Barbara County Water Agency Board of 
Directors would be the decision-making authority for the GSA. The County Board of Supervisors, 
which includes Supervisor Wolf, serves as the ex-officio Board of Directors of the Water Agency. 
The Board would adopt a GSP and will have the powers granted under SOMA, Water Code, section 
I 0725 et seq. In additiof., staff would bring recommendations and requests for olicy guidance to 
the Water Agency Board of Directors as needed. This Board would also set a schedule for regular 
briefings. 
Janet Wolf represents Santa Barbara County's Second Supervisorial District, and assumed 
office on January IO, 2007. The County's Second Supervisorial District includes portions of western 


File No. A-17-194 
Page No. 3 
Santa Barbara, the eastern Goleta Valley as well as neighborhoods from the top of San Marcos Pass 
to the More Mesa and Goleta Beach. 
Supervisor Wolf's personal residence is located on a .76-acre parcel situated within the 
Fringe Area of the Goleta Groundwater Basin. Her residence is served by the Goleta Water District, 
a domestic water supply. She does not have a groundwater well on her property, and you have 
indicated that she has no intent of seeking a permit to instalJ one. According to information 
provided by your office on August 14, 2017, the County is currently responsible for issuing permits 
for well installation. That process would remain unchanged should the GSA be created because the 
GSA would have no permitting authority over wells. Whether or not a GSA is formed, Supervisor 
Wolf would have to follow the same procedure for obtaining a well permit from the County. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from participating in governmental decisions in 
which they have a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial 
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family, or on any of the following: 
(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or 
indirect investment worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or 
indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
(c) Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial 
lending institution made in the regular course of business on terms 
available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating five 
hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, 
received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when 
the decision is made. 
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, 
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. 
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a 
gift or gifts aggregating [$470) or more in value provided to, received 
by, or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time 
when the decision is made. The amount of the value of gifts specified by 
this subdivision shall be adjusted biennially by the commission to equal 
the same amount determined by the commision pursuant to subdivision 
(t) of Section 89503. 
Supervisor Wolf has an interest in her real property (Section p103(b)). 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether or not an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. Under Regulation 1870l(a), an interest is explicitly involved in a decision, 
and presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, when it involves "any governmental decision affecting 


File No. A-17-194 
Page No. 4 
a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)( 1 )-(6)," which detennines 
whether the reasonably foreseeable financial effect on real property is material. 
Relevant to the current matter, Regulation l 8702.2(a)(2) provides the financial effect on real 
property is material whenever the decision "determines the parcel's . . .  inclusion in or exclusion 
from any city, county, district, or other local government subdivision . . .. " Here, the decision would 
include Supervisor Wolfs residential parcel in the GSA for the Fringe Area in the Goleta 
Groundwater Basin. 
However, Regulation 18702(b) provides that notwithstanding a detennination of materiality 
under the materiality standard regulations such as Regulation 18702.2, "the financial effect of a 
government decision is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant. " 
According to your facts, the decision here involves the formation of a Groundwater 
Sustainability Agency for the non-adjudicated fringe areas of the Goleta Groundwater Basin. 
However, Supervisor Wolfs residence is served by the Goleta Water District, she does not have a 
groundwater well, and the creation of the GSA would not prohibit her from installing one. You 
have indicated that the only possible effect of creating the district on Supervisor Wolf is if she had a 
well. Therefore, the mere formation of a GSA would, at most, have "nominal, inconsequential and 
insignificant " financial effects on her property. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Jack C. Woodside 
General Counsel 
(!J, rp)_,t 
By: 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Di"vision

Pursuant to the Political Refonn Act, may Supervisor Wolf make and participate in making 
County decisions regarding the fonnation of the Groundwater Sustainability Agency ("GSA") for 
areas of the Goleta Groundwater Basin where she owns a residential property?

Yes. Supervisor Wolf does not have a prohibited financial interest in the formation of the 
GSA under the Act.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from participating in governmental decisions in 
which they have a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial 
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family, or on any of the following: 
(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or 
indirect investment worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or 
indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
(c) Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial 
lending institution made in the regular course of business on terms 
available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating five 
hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, 
received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when 
the decision is made. 
(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, 
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. 
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a 
gift or gifts aggregating [$470) or more in value provided to, received 
by, or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time 
when the decision is made. The amount of the value of gifts specified by 
this subdivision shall be adjusted biennially by the commission to equal 
the same amount determined by the commision pursuant to subdivision 
(t) of Section 89503. 
Supervisor Wolf has an interest in her real property (Section p103(b)). 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether or not an interest is explicitly 
involved in the decision. Under Regulation 1870l(a), an interest is explicitly involved in a decision,

whether the reasonably foreseeable financial effect on real property is material.

property is material whenever the decision "determines the parcel's . . .  inclusion in or exclusion 
from any city, county, district, or other local government subdivision . . .. " Here, the decision would

However, Regulation 18702(b) provides that notwithstanding a detennination of materiality

According to your facts, the decision here involves the formation of a Groundwater

QUESTION: Pursuant to the Political Refonn Act, may Supervisor Wolf make and participate in making 
County decisions regarding the fonnation of the Groundwater Sustainability Agency ("GSA") for 
areas of the Goleta Groundwater Basin where she owns a residential property?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Supervisor Wolf does not have a prohibited financial interest in the formation of the 
GSA under the Act.
